serving.natural_language_portion: Shredded ginger is typically served as a condiment or garnish in a small portion of approximately 10 g alongside steamed dishes such as Xiao Long Bao.

nutrient_source.title: Ginger root, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169231)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw ginger root used as the primary nutrient reference. The input record cited HPB Singapore with implausibly low macro values (energy 1 kcal/100g, protein 0.02g, carbohydrate 0.18g) that are inconsistent with all approved sources and are likely unit-conversion errors. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy values for raw ginger root are used as the authoritative replacement. Singapore HPB Foodo does not carry a standalone raw ginger entry; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved source available for this ingredient.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for shredded ginger exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on the low-carbohydrate, high-fibre profile of raw ginger root and is consistent with values reported for low-starch root vegetables and spices in Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Glycemic load per 10g serving = (15 × 1.777g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.3, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (13 mg/100g) and high potassium (415 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Shredded ginger is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; its very low sodium content (13 mg/100g) and high potassium content (415 mg/100g) align with DASH diet principles, and gingerols have demonstrated mild vasodilatory effects in peer-reviewed studies — however, high-dose ginger supplements may interact with antihypertensive medications and should be discussed with a physician.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (estimated 15), low sugar (1.7g/100g), and bioactive gingerols with insulin-sensitising properties
type2_diabetes.guidance: Shredded ginger is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its low glycemic index (estimated 15), low sugar content (1.7g/100g), and evidence-based gingerol compounds that improve insulin sensitivity make it a beneficial condiment, though carbohydrate content of 17.77g/100g is moderate and portion size should be kept small (typically 5–15g as a garnish) to maintain negligible glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.20g/100g), zero cholesterol, and anti-inflammatory gingerols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Shredded ginger is suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease; it contains negligible saturated fat (0.20g/100g), zero cholesterol, very low sodium (13 mg/100g), and bioactive gingerols and shogaols that have demonstrated anti-inflammatory and lipid-modulating properties in clinical studies, supporting cardiovascular health when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory gingerols and shogaols inhibit prostaglandin and leukotriene synthesis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ginger is a well-established anti-inflammatory spice; gingerols and shogaols inhibit prostaglandin and leukotriene synthesis, directly reducing joint inflammation, and its very low sodium and absence of saturated fat further support suitability for rheumatoid arthritis — high-dose ginger supplements may interact with RA medications such as methotrexate and should be discussed with a physician.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shredded ginger (姜丝, Jiang si) is fresh ginger root (Zingiber officinale) that has been peeled and cut into fine julienne strips. It is a foundational aromatic condiment in Chinese and broader Southeast Asian cuisine, widely used in Singapore as a garnish and flavour enhancer alongside steamed dishes such as Xiao Long Bao (小笼包), steamed fish, and congee. The flavour profile is pungent, spicy, and warming, with a fibrous yet tender texture when freshly shredded. Nutritionally, raw ginger provides approximately 80 kcal per 100g, with 17.77g carbohydrate, 1.82g protein, and 0.75g fat. It is notably rich in potassium (415 mg/100g) and magnesium (43 mg/100g), and very low in sodium (13 mg/100g), making it compatible with DASH dietary guidelines for hypertension management. Its bioactive compounds — primarily gingerols and shogaols — have well-documented anti-inflammatory, hypoglycaemic, and vasodilatory properties supported by peer-reviewed clinical evidence. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), and a typical 10g condiment serving contributes a negligible glycemic load of approximately 0.3. Ginger is naturally free of cholesterol, gluten, and dairy, and is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, and kosher diets. It is most commonly consumed in small quantities (5–20g) as a garnish or condiment rather than as a primary food, so its per-serving nutritional contribution is modest despite its dense micronutrient profile per 100g.
common_user_queries: Is shredded ginger low in sodium?
What are the health benefits of ginger?
What is the glycemic index of ginger?
Can I eat ginger with Xiao Long Bao?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169231), an approved primary source with exact match to raw ginger root. Input HPB values were identified as implausible unit-conversion errors (energy 1 kcal/100g vs. correct 80 kcal/100g) and replaced with USDA SR Legacy values. GI is an analogous estimate from a peer-reviewed journal (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) as no direct GI measurement exists for shredded ginger in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. INPUT DATA CORRECTION: The input record's per_100g values (energy 1.0 kcal, protein 0.02g, fat 0.01g, carbohydrate 0.18g) are inconsistent with all approved sources and appear to be unit-conversion errors (likely kJ-to-kcal or g-to-mg transposition). These values have been replaced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy values for raw ginger root (FDC ID 169231), which report energy 80 kcal, protein 1.82g, fat 0.75g, carbohydrate 17.77g per 100g. 2. INPUT METADATA CORRECTION: The input record classified ginger under food_group 'Meat and alternatives' and food_subgroup 'Pork-based dishes', which is incorrect. Ginger is a spice/aromatic vegetable and has been reclassified to food_group 'Vegetables', food_subgroup 'Spices and aromatics'. 3. INPUT DESCRIPTION CORRECTION: The input description references supply constraints for Steamed Pork Xiao Long Bao orders, which is a restaurant operational note and not a food record description. This has been replaced with a factual food description. 4. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: vitamin_a_mcg_rae is set to -1 as USDA SR Legacy does not report a RAE value for ginger root in the standard nutrient panel; carotenoid data is not available in the primary source consulted. 5. ZERO VALUES: trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — ginger is a plant food with no trans fats by nature). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant food). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant food contains no B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy). 6. GI ESTIMATION: No direct GI measurement for shredded ginger exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate consistent with low-starch root vegetables and spices per Atkinson et al. (2008). 7. FOOD GROUP RECLASSIFICATION: Input food_group and food_subgroup corrected from pork-based classification to vegetable/spice classification.
